title: The Difference Between AP and Honors Classes explained
author: Tklesson Team
content:
It's course selection season. You see 'Honors' and 'AP' next to class names. Both are harder than regular classes. But they're not the same. Choosing the wrong one can mean unnecessary stress or missed opportunities. Here's what you need to know.

What Are Honors Classes?

Honors classes are more challenging versions of regular high school classes. They cover more material, move faster, and require more critical thinking. But there's no standardized exam at the end. Your grade is based entirely on your teacher's assignments and tests.

Honors classes are designed for students who want a challenge without the pressure of a national exam. They're a great way to show colleges you can handle rigorous work.

Honors classes typically give a GPA boost (e.g., A=4.5 instead of 4.0). The exact amount varies by school. Ask your counselor.

What Are AP Classes?

AP (Advanced Placement) classes are college-level courses taught in high school. At the end of the year, you take a national exam (scored 1-5). A score of 3, 4, or 5 can earn you college credit, depending on the college.

AP classes are standardized across the country. The curriculum is set by the College Board. Your teacher has less flexibility. The AP exam in May is a significant portion of the experience.

AP classes also give a GPA boost, often the same or slightly higher than honors. They are widely recognized by colleges as the most rigorous courses available in high school.

The Key Differences

Content: AP follows a national curriculum. Honors varies by school and teacher.
Exam: AP has a national exam in May. Honors does not.
College credit: AP can earn college credit (if you score well and your college accepts it). Honors cannot.
Stress: AP has a single high-stakes exam. Honors spreads the grade across assignments.
Flexibility: Honors teachers can adjust to student needs. AP teachers are tied to the May exam schedule.

Which One Should You Take?

Take AP if:
• You want potential college credit
• You're applying to very competitive colleges (they expect APs)
• You perform well on standardized tests
• You can handle a year-long build-up to one exam

Take Honors if:
• You want a challenge without the pressure of a national exam
• You're applying to moderately competitive colleges (honors is still impressive)
• You struggle with high-stakes testing
• You want more flexibility in curriculum

Take regular if:
• You're already overwhelmed with other commitments
• You struggled in the prerequisite class
• You need to protect your GPA for college admission

How Many APs Should You Take?

More isn't always better. A student with 3 APs and all A's looks better than a student with 6 APs and a mix of A's, B's, and C's. Quality over quantity. Take APs in subjects you're genuinely interested in and good at.

Most competitive colleges recommend: 0-1 APs as a sophomore, 1-3 APs as a junior, 3-4 APs as a senior. But these are guidelines, not rules. Talk to your counselor about what's realistic for you.

The best schedule is one that challenges you without burning you out. You can't get into college if you're too exhausted to apply.
